source


string_to_dict

 string_to_dict (string, pattern)


f_string = 'I live in {city}, {state}, {country}.'
assert get_formatters_from_string(f_string) == ['city','state','country']


my_string = 'I live in Brooklyn, New York, USA'
string_to_dict(my_string,f_string)

{'city': 'Brooklyn', 'state': 'New York', 'country': 'US'}



source


StringTemplate

 StringTemplate (string=None, template:str)

Custom implementation of Pydantic’s Base Model.
Includes _repr_json_ and _repr_html_ methods for nice displays in Jupyter Lab and Jupyter Notebook, respectively.

class IntroduceMe(StringTemplate):
    template:str = "Hi! My name is {first} {last}"
    first: str
    last: str


IntroduceMe(first='Charlie',last='Schlinkert')


stringHi! My name is Charlie SchlinkerttemplateHi! My name is {first} {last}firstCharlielastSchlinkert



IntroduceMe.parse_string("Hi! My name is Humphry Bogart")


stringHi! My name is Humphry BogarttemplateHi! My name is {first} {last}firstHumphrylastBogart



IntroduceMe("Hi! My name is James Bond")


stringHi! My name is James BondtemplateHi! My name is {first} {last}firstJameslastBond

title: Base Model
text:
Add classmethods to Pandas’ DataFrame object to allow for Pydantic validation


source



 DataFrame (data=None, index:Axes|None=None, columns:Axes|None=None,
            dtype:Dtype|None=None, copy:bool|None=None)

Subclassed from Pandas DataFrame. Includes classmethods used in Pydantic Validation

title: Base Model
text:
DBT Schema

Support functions to convert Pydantic Model into a DBT Schema


source

BaseModel

 BaseModel ()

Custom implementation of Pydantic’s Base Model.
Includes _repr_json_ and _repr_html_ methods for nice displays in Jupyter Lab and Jupyter Notebook, respectively.

import datetime as dt
from pydantic import validator
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
from pydantic import ValidationError


class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    dob: dt.date
    age: Optional[int] 
    
    @validator('age',always=True)
    def _validate_age(cls,v,values):
        difference_in_years = relativedelta(
            dt.date.today(), 
            values['dob']
        ).years
        if v and v!=difference_in_years:
            raise ValueError("You're lying about your age!")
        return difference_in_years


me = Person(
    name = 'Humble Chuck',
    dob = '1994-06-11'
)
me


nameHumble Chuckdob1994-06-11age28



try:
    me = Person(
        name = 'Humble Chuck',
        dob = '1994-06-11',
        age = 27
    )
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)

1 validation error for Person
age
  You're lying about your age! (type=value_error)



source


GenericModel

 GenericModel ()

Custom implementation of Pydantic’s Generic Model.
Includes _repr_json_ and _repr_html_ methods for nice displays in Jupyter Lab and Jupyter Notebook, respectively.

class ModelWithDataFrame(BaseModel):
    df: DataFrame


data = {
    'a':[1],
    'b':[2]
}
dataframe = DataFrame(data) 
model = ModelWithDataFrame(df=dataframe)
model


dfa01b02



model


dfa01b02

title: Base Model
text:
Generic DataFrame Model

Anything passed to ‘data’ will be loaded as a dataframe.


source

DataFrameModel

 DataFrameModel (data:~DataFrameT=None)

Generic DataFrame model. Anything passed to the ‘data’ attribute will be parsed as a DataFrame

from pydantic import HttpUrl,Field
import pandas as pd


class WFUVRecentlyPlayed(DataFrameModel):
    """A DataFrame of recently played songs from WFUV"""
    
    source:ClassVar[HttpUrl] = "https://wfuv.org/playlist"
    timestamp: dt.datetime = Field(description='The time that the data was collected',default_factory = dt.datetime.now)
    
    def __init__(self,*args,**kwargs):
        data = pd.read_html(self.source)[0]
        super().__init__(data=data,*args,**kwargs)


recently_played = WFUVRecentlyPlayed()
recently_played

title: Archetypon
text:
archetypon (pronounced “archetype on”) is a library that uses Pydantic to validate Pandas Dataframes. It includes some usefull extensions of Pydantic’s BaseModel class display models in Jupyter Notebooks and Jupyter Lab.
It’s built using nbdev

title: Record Validation
text:
import datetime as dt


class Model(BaseModel):
    number: int
    string: str 
    date: dt.date
    
dataframe = pd.DataFrame({
    'number':['1',2,3e0],
    'string':['a','b',3],
    'date':['1994-06-11',dt.date.today(),dt.datetime.today()],
},dtype='object')
display(dataframe)
dataframe.dtypes

      3.0

      2023-02-10 14:03:05.866280

display(validated)

bad_dataframe = pd.DataFrame({
    'number':['a',1,'b'],
    'string':dataframe['string'],
    'date':dataframe['date'],
},dtype='object')
try:
    df = parse_dataframe_rows_as(Model,bad_dataframe)
    display(df)
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)

  value is not a valid integer (type=type_error.integer)
__root__ -> number
  value is not a valid integer (type=type_error.integer)



source

record_model

 record_model (kls)

Decorator to make a pydantic model into a RecordFrame, i.e. a DataFrame validated by row.

class ModelWithConstrainedFrame(BaseModel):
    df: RecordFrame[Model]


ModelWithConstrainedFrame(df=dataframe)


dfnumber011223string0a1b23date01994-06-11T00:00:00.00012023-02-10T00:00:00.00022023-02-10T00:00:00.000




Example: Recently Played Songs on WFUV

from archetypon.base_model import DataFrameModel
from pydantic import Field
from pydantic import HttpUrl,Field
import pandas as pd


@record_model
class Song(BaseModel):
    time: str = Field(alias='Time')
    song_title: str = Field(alias='Song Title')
    artist: Optional[str] = Field(alias='Artist')


class WFUVRecentlyPlayed(DataFrameModel[Song]):
    """A DataFrame of recently played songs from WFUV"""
    
    source:ClassVar[HttpUrl] = "https://wfuv.org/playlist"
    timestamp: dt.datetime = Field(description='The time that the data was collected',default_factory = dt.datetime.now)
    
    def __init__(self,*args,**kwargs):
        data = pd.read_html(self.source)[0]
        super().__init__(data=data,*args,**kwargs)


recently_played = WFUVRecentlyPlayed()
recently_played

title: Formatting
text:
source




 format_factory (string_or_callable:~FormatterT, null_format:str='',
                 **kwargs)

Returns a function factory to format a given value.

commas = format_factory("{:,.0f}")
assert commas(1234.0) == '1,234'


def convert_snake_case(string:str,title:bool=False)->str:
    formatted = " ".join(x for x in string.split('_'))
    if title:
        return formatted.title()
    return formatted

snake_to_title = format_factory(convert_snake_case,title=True)
assert snake_to_title("snake_case") == "Snake Case"





source




 big_number (num:float, decimal_places:int=2)


source




 big_dollars (num:float, decimal_places:int=2)


source




 as_multiple (num:float)


assert big_number(1e6) == '1.00M'
assert big_number(1234567)=='1.23M'

assert big_dollars(1e6)=='$1.00M'
assert big_dollars(1234567891) == '$1.23B'

assert as_multiple(1.2) == "1.20x"


source




 format_minutes (time:float)

Takes in minutes as a float and converts to MM:SS




source




 Formatter (null_format:str='', dollars_format:~FormatterT='${:,.0f}',
            percent_format:~FormatterT='{:.0%}',
            percent2dp_format:~FormatterT='{:.2%}',
            number_format:~FormatterT='{:,.0f}',
            small_number_format:~FormatterT='{:.2f}',
            big_number_format:~FormatterT=<function big_number>,
            big_dollars_format:~FormatterT=<function big_dollars>,
            multiple_format:~FormatterT=<function as_multiple>,
            minutes_format:~FormatterT=<function format_minutes>,
            **kwargs)

A customizable object for applying string formats.
Any additional attributes passed during instantion must have a name ending in “_format”. Values can be either a formattable string (e.g. “{:.0f}”) or a callable.



fmt = Formatter(
    # adding custom formats
    date_format = lambda x: pd.Timestamp(x).strftime('%b %d %Y'),
    month_format = "{:%b %Y}",
    millions_format = lambda x: "{:.0f}mm".format(x/1e6),
)



assert fmt.big_dollars(1234567) == big_dollars(1234567) == '$1.23M'
assert fmt.multiple(1.22999)=='1.23x'
assert fmt.percent(.0123)=="1%"
assert fmt.percent2dp(.0123)=="1.23%"
assert fmt.date('20220212')=='Feb 12 2022'
assert fmt.month(dt.date(1994,6,11))=='Jun 1994'
assert fmt.minutes(60.5)=="60:30"
assert fmt.millions(123456789) == "123mm"




The null_format attribute of the Formatter object is a string that gets returned when any method is called on a null value.
By default, nulls are represented as empty strings

import numpy as np


assert fmt.big_dollars(np.nan)==fmt.number(np.nan)==''==fmt.null_format

Formatter will treat empty strings as null.

assert fmt.percent('')==fmt.null_format


custom_null_format = "I'm a null value!"
fmt_custom_null = Formatter(null_format=custom_null_format)
assert fmt_custom_null.big_number(np.nan)==fmt_custom_null.number(np.nan)==custom_null_format

Override default implementation at instantiation:

fmt_custom_dollars = Formatter(
    dollars_format="${:,.2f}"
)
assert fmt_custom_dollars.dollars(12345.678) == "$12,345.68"


fmt_custom = Formatter(
    snake_format = convert_snake_case
)
fmt_custom.snake("snake_case")

'snake case'



# if you need to pass arguments to the function factory 
fmt_custom_with_kwargs = Formatter(
    snake_to_camel_format = (
        convert_snake_case,{'title':True}
    ),
    big_dollars_format = (
        big_dollars,
        {'decimal_places':4}
    )
)
assert fmt_custom_with_kwargs.snake_to_camel("snake_case")=='Snake Case'


assert fmt_custom.big_dollars(1.234e9)=='$1.23B'
assert fmt_custom_with_kwargs.big_dollars(1.1234e9)=='$1.1234B'
